query:
You are working on the Kotlin/kotlinx-datetime repository.
The codebase is at version 0.6 (commit: cff3fdda).

## Issue to Resolve

Instant.parse(..) inconsistent between jvm and ios targets
From [ISO_DATE_TIME_OFFSET format](https://kotlinlang.org/api/kotlinx-datetime/kotlinx-datetime/kotlinx.datetime.format/-date-time-components/-formats/-i-s-o_-d-a-t-e_-t-i-m-e_-o-f-f-s-e-t.html):

> .., except that during parsing, specifying the minutes is optional.
>
> This format differs from [LocalTime.Formats.ISO](https://kotlinlang.org/api/kotlinx-datetime/kotlinx-datetime/kotlinx.datetime/-local-time/-formats/-i-s-o.html) in its time part in that specifying the seconds is not optional.

JVM allows to ommit seconds (i.e. parse "2024-03-19T17:50Z") while builds for ios do not (fails with `kotlinx.datetime.DateTimeFormatException: Parse error at char 16: expected char ':', got 'Z`)

Omitting minutes, while allowed as per documentation, always fails for both targets.

## Additional Context from Issue Discussion

Thanks! The issue is that, on the JVM and the JS, we delegate to the platform-specific implementation of `OffsetDateTime.parse`, which does allow skipping the seconds: https://docs.oracle.com/javase/8/docs/api/java/time/OffsetDateTime.html#parse-java.lang.CharSequence- We do that to work around the older JVM versions not supporting parsing arbitrary offsets, only `Z`.

The solution seems to be to stop relying on the platform implementation and use our version of `ISO_DATE_TIME_OFFSET` throughout.

Also, "omitting minutes" refers to the minutes of the UTC offset, that is, you can just have `+03` instead of `+03:00`.
regarding minutes: my bad, can't read 🤦 

It would be nice if both targets would behave equally, especially since folks tend to test on jvm/android and just expect (or hope) that ios will just work the same way. But, if read correctly, the documentation is right. Generally it is not allowed to ommit the seconds part.

## Relevant Files

The following files are likely relevant to solving this issue:
- `core/common/src/format/DateTimeComponents.kt`
- `core/commonJs/src/Instant.kt`
- `core/jvm/src/Instant.kt`

## Instructions

Please analyze this issue and implement a fix. Make the necessary code changes to resolve the problem described above.

Focus on:
- Understanding the root cause of the issue
- Making minimal, targeted changes to fix the problem
- Ensuring your changes don't break existing functionality

## Important Evaluation Notes

**Your work will be evaluated by running hidden tests that you do not have access to.** These tests will verify that your fix correctly addresses the issue. You should be confident in your solution before finishing.
**If you write tests to verify your changes, create them in NEW test files only** - do NOT modify existing test files

## CRITICAL RESTRICTIONS

**NEVER use git commands.** Do not run `git status`, `git diff`, `git checkout`, `git reset`, or ANY other git commands. Using git will corrupt the evaluation environment and invalidate your entire submission. All file operations should be done through normal file editing and reading tools, not git.

1→/*
  2→ * Copyright 2019-2020 JetBrains s.r.o.
  3→ * Use of this source code is governed by the Apache 2.0 License that can be found in the LICENSE.txt file.
  4→ */
  5→
  6→package kotlinx.datetime
  7→
  8→import kotlinx.datetime.format.*
  9→import kotlinx.datetime.internal.*
 10→import kotlinx.datetime.serializers.InstantIso8601Serializer
 11→import kotlinx.datetime.serializers.InstantComponentSerializer
 12→import kotlinx.serialization.Serializable
 13→import kotlin.time.*
 14→
 15→/**
 16→ * A moment in time.
 17→ *
 18→ * A point in time must be uniquely identified so that it is independent of a time zone.
 19→ * For example, `1970-01-01, 00:00:00` does not represent a moment in time since this would happen at different times
 20→ * in different time zones: someone in Tokyo would think it is already `1970-01-01` several hours earlier than someone in
 21→ * Berlin would. To represent such entities, use [LocalDateTime].
 22→ * In contrast, "the moment the clocks in London first showed 00:00 on Jan 1, 2000" is a specific moment
 23→ * in time, as is "1970-01-01, 00:00:00 UTC+0", so it can be represented as an [Instant].
 24→ *
 25→ * `Instant` uses the UTC-SLS (smeared leap second) time scale. This time scale doesn't contain instants
 26→ * corresponding to leap seconds, but instead "smears" positive and negative leap seconds among the last 1000 seconds
 27→ * of the day when a leap second happens.
 28→ *
 29→ * ### Obtaining the current moment
 30→ *
 31→ * The [Clock] interface is the primary way to obtain the current moment:
 32→ *
 33→ * ```
 34→ * val clock: Clock = Clock.System
 35→ * val instant = clock.now()
 36→ * ```
 37→ *
 38→ * The [Clock.System] implementation uses the platform-specific system clock to obtain the current moment.
 39→ * Note that this clock is not guaranteed to be monotonic, and it may be adjusted by the user or the system at any time,
 40→ * so it should not be used for measuring time intervals.
 41→ * For that, consider using [TimeSource.Monotonic] and [TimeMark] instead of [Clock.System] and [Instant].
 42→ *
 43→ * ### Obtaining human-readable representations
 44→ *
 45→ * #### Date and time
 46→ *
 47→ * [Instant] is essentially the number of seconds and nanoseconds since a designated moment in time,
 48→ * stored as something like `1709898983.123456789`.
 49→ * [Instant] does not contain information about the day or time, as this depends on the time zone.
 50→ * To work with this information for a specific time zone, obtain a [LocalDateTime] using [Instant.toLocalDateTime]:
 51→ *
 52→ * ```
 53→ * val instant = Instant.fromEpochSeconds(1709898983, 123456789)
 54→ * instant.toLocalDateTime(TimeZone.of("Europe/Berlin")) // 2024-03-08T12:56:23.123456789
 55→ * instant.toLocalDateTime(TimeZone.UTC) // 2024-03-08T11:56:23.123456789
 56→ * ```
 57→ *
 58→ * For values very far in the past or the future, this conversion may fail.
 59→ * The specific range of values that can be converted to [LocalDateTime] is platform-specific, but at least
 60→ * [DISTANT_PAST], [DISTANT_FUTURE], and all values between them can be converted to [LocalDateTime].
 61→ *
 62→ * #### Date or time separately
 63→ *
 64→ * To obtain a [LocalDate] or [LocalTime], first, obtain a [LocalDateTime], and then use its [LocalDateTime.date]
 65→ * and [LocalDateTime.time] properties:
 66→ *
 67→ * ```
 68→ * val instant = Instant.fromEpochSeconds(1709898983, 123456789)
 69→ * instant.toLocalDateTime(TimeZone.of("Europe/Berlin")).date // 2024-03-08
 70→ * ```
 71→ *
 72→ * ### Arithmetic operations
 73→ *
 74→ * #### Elapsed-time-based
 75→ *
 76→ * The [plus] and [minus] operators can be used to add [Duration]s to and subtract them from an [Instant]:
 77→ *
 78→ * ```
 79→ * Clock.System.now() + 5.seconds // 5 seconds from now
 80→ * ```
 81→ *
 82→ * Durations can also be represented as multiples of some [time-based date-time unit][DateTimeUnit.TimeBased]:
 83→ *
 84→ * ```
 85→ * Clock.System.now().plus(4, DateTimeUnit.HOUR) // 4 hours from now
 86→ * ```
 87→ *
 88→ * Also, there is a [minus] operator that returns the [Duration] representing the difference between two instants:
 89→ *
 90→ * ```
 91→ * val start = Clock.System.now()
 92→ * val concertStart = LocalDateTime(2023, 1, 1, 20, 0, 0).toInstant(TimeZone.of("Europe/Berlin"))
 93→ * val timeUntilConcert = concertStart - start
 94→ * ```
 95→ *
 96→ * #### Calendar-based
 97→ *
 98→ * Since [Instant] represents a point in time, it is always well-defined what the result of arithmetic operations on it
 99→ * is, including the cases when a calendar is used.
100→ * This is not the case for [LocalDateTime], where the result of arithmetic operations depends on the time zone.
101→ * See the [LocalDateTime] documentation for more details.
102→ *
103→ * Adding and subtracting calendar-based units can be done using the [plus] and [minus] operators,
104→ * requiring a [TimeZone]:
105→ *
106→ * ```
107→ * // One day from now in Berlin
108→ * Clock.System.now().plus(1, DateTimeUnit.DAY, TimeZone.of("Europe/Berlin"))
109→ *
110→ * // A day and two hours short from two months later in Berlin
111→ * Clock.System.now().plus(DateTimePeriod(months = 2, days = -1, hours = -2), TimeZone.of("Europe/Berlin"))
112→ * ```
113→ *
114→ * The difference between [Instant] values in terms of calendar-based units can be obtained using the [periodUntil]
115→ * method:
116→ *
117→ * ```
118→ * val start = Clock.System.now()
119→ * val concertStart = LocalDateTime(2023, 1, 1, 20, 0, 0).toInstant(TimeZone.of("Europe/Berlin"))
120→ * val timeUntilConcert = start.periodUntil(concertStart, TimeZone.of("Europe/Berlin"))
121→ * // Two months, three days, four hours, and five minutes until the concert
122→ * ```
123→ *
124→ * or [Instant.until] method, as well as [Instant.daysUntil], [Instant.monthsUntil],
125→ * and [Instant.yearsUntil] extension functions:
126→ *
127→ * ```
128→ * val start = Clock.System.now()
129→ * val concertStart = LocalDateTime(2023, 1, 1, 20, 0, 0).toInstant(TimeZone.of("Europe/Berlin"))
130→ * val timeUntilConcert = start.until(concertStart, DateTimeUnit.DAY, TimeZone.of("Europe/Berlin"))
131→ * // 63 days until the concert, rounded down
132→ * ```
133→ *
134→ * ### Platform specifics
135→ *
136→ * On the JVM, there are `Instant.toJavaInstant()` and `java.time.Instant.toKotlinInstant()`
137→ * extension functions to convert between `kotlinx.datetime` and `java.time` objects used for the same purpose.
138→ * Similarly, on the Darwin platforms, there are `Instant.toNSDate()` and `NSDate.toKotlinInstant()`
139→ * extension functions.
140→ *
141→ * ### Construction, serialization, and deserialization
142→ *
143→ * [fromEpochSeconds] can be used to construct an instant from the number of seconds since
144→ * `1970-01-01T00:00:00Z` (the Unix epoch).
145→ * [epochSeconds] and [nanosecondsOfSecond] can be used to obtain the number of seconds and nanoseconds since the epoch.
146→ *
147→ * ```
148→ * val instant = Instant.fromEpochSeconds(1709898983, 123456789)
149→ * instant.epochSeconds // 1709898983
150→ * instant.nanosecondsOfSecond // 123456789
151→ * ```
152→ *
153→ * [fromEpochMilliseconds] allows constructing an instant from the number of milliseconds since the epoch.
154→ * [toEpochMilliseconds] can be used to obtain the number of milliseconds since the epoch.
155→ * Note that [Instant] supports nanosecond precision, so converting to milliseconds is a lossy operation.
156→ *
157→ * ```
158→ * val instant1 = Instant.fromEpochSeconds(1709898983, 123456789)
159→ * instant1.nanosecondsOfSecond // 123456789
160→ * val milliseconds = instant1.toEpochMilliseconds() // 1709898983123
161→ * val instant2 = Instant.fromEpochMilliseconds(milliseconds)
162→ * instant2.nanosecondsOfSecond // 123000000
163→ * ```
164→ *
165→ * [parse] and [toString] methods can be used to obtain an [Instant] from and convert it to a string in the
166→ * ISO 8601 extended format.
167→ *
168→ * ```
169→ * val instant = Instant.parse("2023-01-02T22:35:01+01:00")
170→ * instant.toString() // 2023-01-02T21:35:01Z
171→ * ```
172→ *
173→ * During parsing, the UTC offset is not returned separately.
174→ * If the UTC offset is important, use [DateTimeComponents] with [DateTimeComponents.Formats.ISO_DATE_TIME_OFFSET] to
175→ * parse the string instead.
176→ *
177→ * [Instant.parse] and [Instant.format] also accept custom formats:
178→ *
179→ * ```
180→ * val customFormat = DateTimeComponents.Format {
181→ *   date(LocalDate.Formats.ISO)
182→ *   char(' ')
183→ *   time(LocalTime.Formats.ISO)
184→ *   char(' ')
185→ *   offset(UtcOffset.Formats.ISO)
186→ * }
187→ * val instant = Instant.parse("2023-01-02 22:35:01.14 +01:00", customFormat)
188→ * instant.format(customFormat, offset = UtcOffset(hours = 2)) // 2023-01-02 23:35:01.14 +02:00
189→ * ```
190→ *
191→ * Additionally, there are several `kotlinx-serialization` serializers for [Instant]:
192→ * - [InstantIso8601Serializer] for the ISO 8601 extended format.
193→ * - [InstantComponentSerializer] for an object with components.
194→ *
195→ * @see LocalDateTime for a user-visible representation of moments in time in an unspecified time zone.
196→ */
197→@Serializable(with = InstantIso8601Serializer::class)
198→public expect class Instant : Comparable<Instant> {
199→
200→    /**
201→     * The number of seconds from the epoch instant `1970-01-01T00:00:00Z` rounded down to a [Long] number.
202→     *
203→     * The difference between the rounded number of seconds and the actual number of seconds
204→     * is returned by [nanosecondsOfSecond] property expressed in nanoseconds.
205→     *
206→     * Note that this number doesn't include leap seconds added or removed since the epoch.
207→     *
208→     * @see fromEpochSeconds
209→     * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.InstantSamples.epochSeconds
210→     */
211→    public val epochSeconds: Long
212→
213→    /**
214→     * The number of nanoseconds by which this instant is later than [epochSeconds] from the epoch instant.
215→     *
216→     * The value is always non-negative and lies in the range `0..999_999_999`.
217→     *
218→     * @see fromEpochSeconds
219→     * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.InstantSamples.nanosecondsOfSecond
220→     */
221→    public val nanosecondsOfSecond: Int
222→
223→    /**
224→     * Returns the number of milliseconds from the epoch instant `1970-01-01T00:00:00Z`.
225→     *
226→     * Any fractional part of millisecond is rounded toward zero to the whole number of milliseconds.
227→     *
228→     * If the result does not fit in [Long], returns [Long.MAX_VALUE] for a positive result or [Long.MIN_VALUE] for a negative result.
229→     *
230→     * @see fromEpochMilliseconds
231→     * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.InstantSamples.toEpochMilliseconds
232→     */
233→    public fun toEpochMilliseconds(): Long
234→
235→    /**
236→     * Returns an instant that is the result of adding the specified [duration] to this instant.
237→     *
238→     * If the [duration] is positive, the returned instant is later than this instant.
239→     * If the [duration] is negative, the returned instant is earlier than this instant.
240→     *
241→     * The return value is clamped to the platform-specific boundaries for [Instant] if the result exceeds them.
242→     *
243→     * **Pitfall**: [Duration.Companion.days] are multiples of 24 hours and are not calendar-based.
244→     * Consider using the [plus] overload that accepts a multiple of a [DateTimeUnit] instead for calendar-based
245→     * operations instead of using [Duration].
246→     * For an explanation of why some days are not 24 hours, see [DateTimeUnit.DayBased].
247→     *
248→     * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.InstantSamples.plusDuration
249→     */
250→    public operator fun plus(duration: Duration): Instant
251→
252→    /**
253→     * Returns an instant that is the result of subtracting the specified [duration] from this instant.
254→     *
255→     * If the [duration] is positive, the returned instant is earlier than this instant.
256→     * If the [duration] is negative, the returned instant is later than this instant.
257→     *

259→     *

261→     * Consider using the [minus] overload that accepts a multiple of a [DateTimeUnit] instead for calendar-based
262→     * operations instead of using [Duration].
263→     * For an explanation of why some days are not 24 hours, see [DateTimeUnit.DayBased].
264→     *

267→    public operator fun minus(duration: Duration): Instant

270→    /**
271→     * Returns the [Duration] between two instants: [other] and `this`.
272→     *
273→     * The duration returned is positive if this instant is later than the other,
274→     * and negative if this instant is earlier than the other.
275→     *
276→     * The result is never clamped, but note that for instants that are far apart,
277→     * the value returned may represent the duration between them inexactly due to the loss of precision.
278→     *
279→     * Note that sources of [Instant] values (in particular, [Clock]) are not guaranteed to be in sync with each other
280→     * or even monotonic, so the result of this operation may be negative even if the other instant was observed later
281→     * than this one, or vice versa.
282→     * For measuring time intervals, consider using [TimeSource.Monotonic].
283→     *
284→     * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.InstantSamples.minusInstant
285→     */
286→    public operator fun minus(other: Instant): Duration

291→     * a negative number if this instant is earlier than the other,

298→    /**
299→     * Converts this instant to the ISO 8601 string representation, for example, `2023-01-02T23:40:57.120Z`.
300→     *
301→     * The representation uses the UTC-SLS time scale instead of UTC.
302→     * In practice, this means that leap second handling will not be readjusted to the UTC.
303→     * Leap seconds will not be added or skipped, so it is impossible to acquire a string
304→     * where the component for seconds is 60, and for any day, it's possible to observe 23:59:59.
305→     *
306→     * @see parse
307→     * @see DateTimeComponents.Formats.ISO_DATE_TIME_OFFSET for a very similar format. The difference is that
308→     * [DateTimeComponents.Formats.ISO_DATE_TIME_OFFSET] will not add trailing zeros for readability to the
309→     * fractional part of the second.

311→     */
312→    public override fun toString(): String

317→        public fun now(): Instant

319→        /**

321→         *

323→         * In any case, it is guaranteed that instants between [DISTANT_PAST] and [DISTANT_FUTURE] can be represented.
324→         *

334→         * and the [nanosecondAdjustment] number of nanoseconds from the whole second.
335→         *
336→         * The return value is clamped to the platform-specific boundaries for [Instant] if the result exceeds them.
337→         * In any case, it is guaranteed that instants between [DISTANT_PAST] and [DISTANT_FUTURE] can be represented.
338→         *
339→         * [fromEpochMilliseconds] is a similar function for when input data only has millisecond precision.
340→         *
341→         * @see Instant.epochSeconds
342→         * @see Instant.nanosecondsOfSecond
343→         * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.InstantSamples.fromEpochSeconds
344→         */

346→
347→        /**

349→         * and the [nanosecondAdjustment] number of nanoseconds from the whole second.
350→         *
351→         * The return value is clamped to the platform-specific boundaries for [Instant] if the result exceeds them.
352→         * In any case, it is guaranteed that instants between [DISTANT_PAST] and [DISTANT_FUTURE] can be represented.
353→         *
354→         * [fromEpochMilliseconds] is a similar function for when input data only has millisecond precision.
355→         *

357→         * @see Instant.nanosecondsOfSecond
358→         * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.InstantSamples.fromEpochSecondsIntNanos
359→         */
360→        public fun fromEpochSeconds(epochSeconds: Long, nanosecondAdjustment: Int): Instant
361→
362→        /**
363→         * A shortcut for calling [DateTimeFormat.parse], followed by [DateTimeComponents.toInstantUsingOffset].
364→         *
365→         * Parses a string that represents an instant including date and time components and a mandatory
366→         * time zone offset and returns the parsed [Instant] value.
367→         *
368→         * The string is considered to represent time on the UTC-SLS time scale instead of UTC.
369→         * In practice, this means that, even if there is a leap second on the given day, it will not affect how the
370→         * time is parsed, even if it's in the last 1000 seconds of the day.
371→         * Instead, even if there is a negative leap second on the given day, 23:59:59 is still considered valid time.
372→         * 23:59:60 is invalid on UTC-SLS, so parsing it will fail.
373→         *
374→         * If the format is not specified, [DateTimeComponents.Formats.ISO_DATE_TIME_OFFSET] is used.
375→         * `2023-01-02T23:40:57.120Z` is an example of a string in this format.
376→         *
377→         * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the text cannot be parsed or the boundaries of [Instant] are exceeded.
378→         *
379→         * @see Instant.toString for formatting using the default format.
380→         * @see Instant.format for formatting using a custom format.
381→         * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.InstantSamples.parsing
382→         */
383→        public fun parse(
384→            input: CharSequence,
385→            format: DateTimeFormat<DateTimeComponents> = DateTimeComponents.Formats.ISO_DATE_TIME_OFFSET
386→        ): Instant

390→         *
391→         * All instants in the range `DISTANT_PAST..DISTANT_FUTURE` can be [converted][Instant.toLocalDateTime] to
392→         * [LocalDateTime] without exceptions in every time zone on all supported platforms.
393→         *
394→         * [isDistantPast] returns true for this value and all earlier ones.

398→        /**

403→         *
404→         * [isDistantFuture] returns true for this value and all later ones.
405→         */
406→        public val DISTANT_FUTURE: Instant // +100000-01-01T00:00:00Z
407→
408→        internal val MIN: Instant
409→        internal val MAX: Instant

435→/**
436→ * Returns an instant that is the result of adding components of [DateTimePeriod] to this instant. The components are
437→ * added in the order from the largest units to the smallest, i.e., from years to nanoseconds.
438→ *
439→ * - If the [DateTimePeriod] only contains time-based components, please consider adding a [Duration] instead,
440→ *   as in `Clock.System.now() + 5.hours`.
441→ *   Then, it will not be necessary to pass the [timeZone].
442→ * - If the [DateTimePeriod] only has a single non-zero component (only the months or only the days),
443→ *   please consider using a multiple of [DateTimeUnit.DAY] or [DateTimeUnit.MONTH], like in
444→ *   `Clock.System.now().plus(5, DateTimeUnit.DAY, TimeZone.currentSystemDefault())`.
445→ *
446→ * @throws DateTimeArithmeticException if this value or the results of intermediate computations are too large to fit in
447→ * [LocalDateTime].
448→ * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.InstantSamples.plusPeriod
449→ */
450→public expect fun Instant.plus(period: DateTimePeriod, timeZone: TimeZone): Instant
451→
452→/**
453→ * Returns an instant that is the result of subtracting components of [DateTimePeriod] from this instant. The components
454→ * are subtracted in the order from the largest units to the smallest, i.e., from years to nanoseconds.
455→ *
456→ * - If the [DateTimePeriod] only contains time-based components, please consider subtracting a [Duration] instead,
457→ *   as in `Clock.System.now() - 5.hours`.
458→ *   Then, it is not necessary to pass the [timeZone].
459→ * - If the [DateTimePeriod] only has a single non-zero component (only the months or only the days),
460→ *   please consider using a multiple of [DateTimeUnit.DAY] or [DateTimeUnit.MONTH], as in
461→ *   `Clock.System.now().minus(5, DateTimeUnit.DAY, TimeZone.currentSystemDefault())`.
462→ *
463→ * @throws DateTimeArithmeticException if this value or the results of intermediate computations are too large to fit in
464→ * [LocalDateTime].
465→ * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.InstantSamples.minusPeriod
466→ */
467→public fun Instant.minus(period: DateTimePeriod, timeZone: TimeZone): Instant =
468→    /* An overflow can happen for any component, but we are only worried about nanoseconds, as having an overflow in
469→    any other component means that `plus` will throw due to the minimum value of the numeric type overflowing the
470→    platform-specific limits. */
471→    if (period.totalNanoseconds != Long.MIN_VALUE) {
472→        val negatedPeriod = with(period) { buildDateTimePeriod(-totalMonths, -days, -totalNanoseconds) }
473→        plus(negatedPeriod, timeZone)
474→    } else {
475→        val negatedPeriod = with(period) { buildDateTimePeriod(-totalMonths, -days, -(totalNanoseconds+1)) }
476→        plus(negatedPeriod, timeZone).plus(1, DateTimeUnit.NANOSECOND)
477→    }

479→/**
480→ * Returns a [DateTimePeriod] representing the difference between `this` and [other] instants.
481→ *
482→ * The components of [DateTimePeriod] are calculated so that adding it to `this` instant results in the [other] instant.
483→ *
484→ * All components of the [DateTimePeriod] returned are:
485→ * - Positive or zero if this instant is earlier than the other.
486→ * - Negative or zero if this instant is later than the other.
487→ * - Exactly zero if this instant is equal to the other.
488→ *
489→ * @throws DateTimeArithmeticException if `this` or [other] instant is too large to fit in [LocalDateTime].
490→ *     Or (only on the JVM) if the number of months between the two dates exceeds an Int.
491→ * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.InstantSamples.periodUntil
492→ */

495→/**
496→ * Returns the whole number of the specified date or time [units][unit] between `this` and [other] instants
497→ * in the specified [timeZone].
498→ *
499→ * The value returned is:
500→ * - Positive or zero if this instant is earlier than the other.
501→ * - Negative or zero if this instant is later than the other.
502→ * - Zero if this instant is equal to the other.
503→ *
504→ * If the result does not fit in [Long], returns [Long.MAX_VALUE] for a positive result or [Long.MIN_VALUE] for a negative result.
505→ *
506→ * @throws DateTimeArithmeticException if `this` or [other] instant is too large to fit in [LocalDateTime].
507→ * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.InstantSamples.untilAsDateTimeUnit
508→ */
509→public expect fun Instant.until(other: Instant, unit: DateTimeUnit, timeZone: TimeZone): Long
510→
511→/**
512→ * Returns the whole number of the specified time [units][unit] between `this` and [other] instants.
513→ *
514→ * The value returned is:
515→ * - Positive or zero if this instant is earlier than the other.
516→ * - Negative or zero if this instant is later than the other.
517→ * - Zero if this instant is equal to the other.
518→ *
519→ * If the result does not fit in [Long], returns [Long.MAX_VALUE] for a positive result or [Long.MIN_VALUE] for a negative result.
520→ *
521→ * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.InstantSamples.untilAsTimeBasedUnit
522→ */
523→public fun Instant.until(other: Instant, unit: DateTimeUnit.TimeBased): Long =
524→    try {
525→        multiplyAddAndDivide(other.epochSeconds - epochSeconds,
526→            NANOS_PER_ONE.toLong(),
527→            (other.nanosecondsOfSecond - nanosecondsOfSecond).toLong(),
528→            unit.nanoseconds)
529→    } catch (e: ArithmeticException) {
530→        if (this < other) Long.MAX_VALUE else Long.MIN_VALUE
531→    }

765→ * @throws DateTimeArithmeticException if `this` or [other] instant is too large to fit in [LocalDateTime].
766→ * @see Instant.until for the same operation but with swapped arguments.
767→ * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.InstantSamples.minusAsDateTimeUnit
768→ */
769→public fun Instant.minus(other: Instant, unit: DateTimeUnit, timeZone: TimeZone): Long =
770→        other.until(this, unit, timeZone)
771→
772→/**
773→ * Returns the whole number of the specified time [units][unit] between [other] and `this` instants.
774→ *
775→ * The value returned is negative or zero if this instant is earlier than the other,
776→ * and positive or zero if this instant is later than the other.
777→ *
778→ * If the result does not fit in [Long], returns [Long.MAX_VALUE] for a positive result or [Long.MIN_VALUE] for a negative result.
779→ *
780→ * @see Instant.until for the same operation but with swapped arguments.
781→ * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.InstantSamples.minusAsTimeBasedUnit
782→ */
783→public fun Instant.minus(other: Instant, unit: DateTimeUnit.TimeBased): Long =
784→    other.until(this, unit)
785→
786→/**
787→ * Formats this value using the given [format] using the given [offset].
788→ *
789→ * Equivalent to calling [DateTimeFormat.format] on [format] and using [DateTimeComponents.setDateTimeOffset] in
790→ * the lambda.
791→ *
792→ * [DateTimeComponents.Formats.ISO_DATE_TIME_OFFSET] is a format very similar to the one used by [toString].
793→ * The only difference is that [Instant.toString] adds trailing zeros to the fraction-of-second component so that the
794→ * number of digits after a dot is a multiple of three.
795→ *
796→ * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.InstantSamples.formatting
797→ */
798→public fun Instant.format(format: DateTimeFormat<DateTimeComponents>, offset: UtcOffset = UtcOffset.ZERO): String {
799→    val instant = this
800→    return format.format { setDateTimeOffset(instant, offset) }
801→}

804→internal const val DISTANT_FUTURE_SECONDS = 3093527980800

  5→@file:JvmName("InstantJvmKt")

  7→package kotlinx.datetime
  8→
  9→import kotlinx.datetime.format.*
 10→import kotlinx.datetime.internal.safeMultiply
 11→import kotlinx.datetime.internal.*
 12→import kotlinx.datetime.serializers.InstantIso8601Serializer
 13→import kotlinx.serialization.Serializable
 14→import java.time.DateTimeException
 15→import java.time.format.DateTimeParseException
 16→import java.time.temporal.ChronoUnit
 17→import kotlin.time.*
 18→import kotlin.time.Duration.Companion.nanoseconds
 19→import kotlin.time.Duration.Companion.seconds
 20→import java.time.Instant as jtInstant
 21→import java.time.OffsetDateTime as jtOffsetDateTime
 22→import java.time.Clock as jtClock
 23→
 24→@Serializable(with = InstantIso8601Serializer::class)
 25→public actual class Instant internal constructor(internal val value: jtInstant) : Comparable<Instant> {
 26→
 27→    public actual val epochSeconds: Long
 28→        get() = value.epochSecond
 29→    public actual val nanosecondsOfSecond: Int
 30→        get() = value.nano
 31→
 32→    public actual fun toEpochMilliseconds(): Long = try {
 33→        value.toEpochMilli()
 34→    } catch (e: ArithmeticException) {
 35→        if (value.isAfter(java.time.Instant.EPOCH)) Long.MAX_VALUE else Long.MIN_VALUE
 36→    }
 37→
 38→    public actual operator fun plus(duration: Duration): Instant = duration.toComponents { seconds, nanoseconds ->
 39→        try {
 40→            Instant(value.plusSeconds(seconds).plusNanos(nanoseconds.toLong()))
 41→        } catch (e: java.lang.Exception) {
 42→            if (e !is ArithmeticException && e !is DateTimeException) throw e
 43→            if (duration.isPositive()) MAX else MIN
 44→        }
 45→    }
 46→
 47→    public actual operator fun minus(duration: Duration): Instant = plus(-duration)
 48→
 49→    public actual operator fun minus(other: Instant): Duration =
 50→        (this.value.epochSecond - other.value.epochSecond).seconds + // won't overflow given the instant bounds
 51→            (this.value.nano - other.value.nano).nanoseconds
 52→
 53→    public actual override operator fun compareTo(other: Instant): Int = this.value.compareTo(other.value)
 54→
 55→    override fun equals(other: Any?): Boolean =
 56→            (this === other) || (other is Instant && this.value == other.value)
 57→
 58→    override fun hashCode(): Int = value.hashCode()
 59→
 60→    actual override fun toString(): String = value.toString()
 61→
 62→    public actual companion object {
 63→        @Deprecated("Use Clock.System.now() instead", ReplaceWith("Clock.System.now()", "kotlinx.datetime.Clock"), level = DeprecationLevel.ERROR)
 64→        public actual fun now(): Instant =
 65→                Instant(jtClock.systemUTC().instant())
 66→
 67→        public actual fun fromEpochMilliseconds(epochMilliseconds: Long): Instant =
 68→                Instant(jtInstant.ofEpochMilli(epochMilliseconds))
 69→
 70→        public actual fun parse(input: CharSequence, format: DateTimeFormat<DateTimeComponents>): Instant =
 71→            if (format === DateTimeComponents.Formats.ISO_DATE_TIME_OFFSET) {
 72→                try {
 73→                    Instant(jtOffsetDateTime.parse(fixOffsetRepresentation(input)).toInstant())
 74→                } catch (e: DateTimeParseException) {
 75→                    throw DateTimeFormatException(e)
 76→                }
 77→            } else {
 78→                try {
 79→                    format.parse(input).toInstantUsingOffset()
 80→                } catch (e: IllegalArgumentException) {
 81→                    throw DateTimeFormatException("Failed to parse an instant from '$input'", e)
 82→                }
 83→            }
 84→
 85→        @Deprecated("This overload is only kept for binary compatibility", level = DeprecationLevel.HIDDEN)
 86→        public fun parse(isoString: String): Instant = parse(input = isoString)
 87→
 88→        /** A workaround for a quirk of the JDKs older than 11 where the string representations of Instant that have an
 89→         * offset of the form "+XX" are not recognized by [jtOffsetDateTime.parse], while "+XX:XX" work fine. */
 90→        private fun fixOffsetRepresentation(isoString: CharSequence): CharSequence {
 91→            val time = isoString.indexOf('T', ignoreCase = true)
 92→            if (time == -1) return isoString // the string is malformed
 93→            val offset = isoString.indexOfLast { c -> c == '+' || c == '-' }
 94→            if (offset < time) return isoString // the offset is 'Z' and not +/- something else
 95→            val separator = isoString.indexOf(':', offset) // if there is a ':' in the offset, no changes needed
 96→            return if (separator != -1) isoString else "$isoString:00"
 97→        }
 98→
 99→        public actual fun fromEpochSeconds(epochSeconds: Long, nanosecondAdjustment: Long): Instant = try {
100→            Instant(jtInstant.ofEpochSecond(epochSeconds, nanosecondAdjustment))
101→        } catch (e: Exception) {
102→            if (e !is ArithmeticException && e !is DateTimeException) throw e
103→            if (epochSeconds > 0) MAX else MIN
104→        }
105→
106→        public actual fun fromEpochSeconds(epochSeconds: Long, nanosecondAdjustment: Int): Instant =
107→            fromEpochSeconds(epochSeconds, nanosecondAdjustment.toLong())
108→
109→        public actual val DISTANT_PAST: Instant = Instant(jtInstant.ofEpochSecond(DISTANT_PAST_SECONDS, 999_999_999))
110→        public actual val DISTANT_FUTURE: Instant = Instant(jtInstant.ofEpochSecond(DISTANT_FUTURE_SECONDS, 0))
111→
112→        internal actual val MIN: Instant = Instant(jtInstant.MIN)
113→        internal actual val MAX: Instant = Instant(jtInstant.MAX)
114→    }
115→}
116→
117→private fun Instant.atZone(zone: TimeZone): java.time.ZonedDateTime = try {
118→    value.atZone(zone.zoneId)
119→} catch (e: DateTimeException) {
120→    throw DateTimeArithmeticException(e)
121→}
122→
123→public actual fun Instant.plus(period: DateTimePeriod, timeZone: TimeZone): Instant {
124→    try {
125→        val thisZdt = atZone(timeZone)
126→        return with(period) {
127→            thisZdt
128→                    .run { if (totalMonths != 0) plusMonths(totalMonths.toLong()) else this }
129→                    .run { if (days != 0) plusDays(days.toLong()) else this }
130→                    .run { if (totalNanoseconds != 0L) plusNanos(totalNanoseconds) else this }
131→        }.toInstant().let(::Instant)
132→    } catch (e: DateTimeException) {
133→        throw DateTimeArithmeticException(e)
134→    }
135→}
136→
137→@Deprecated("Use the plus overload with an explicit number of units", ReplaceWith("this.plus(1, unit, timeZone)"))
138→public actual fun Instant.plus(unit: DateTimeUnit, timeZone: TimeZone): Instant =
139→        plus(1L, unit, timeZone)
140→
141→public actual fun Instant.plus(value: Int, unit: DateTimeUnit, timeZone: TimeZone): Instant =
142→        plus(value.toLong(), unit, timeZone)
143→
144→public actual fun Instant.minus(value: Int, unit: DateTimeUnit, timeZone: TimeZone): Instant =
145→        plus(-value.toLong(), unit, timeZone)
146→
147→public actual fun Instant.plus(value: Long, unit: DateTimeUnit, timeZone: TimeZone): Instant =
148→        try {
149→            val thisZdt = atZone(timeZone)
150→            when (unit) {
151→                is DateTimeUnit.TimeBased ->
152→                    plus(value, unit).value.also { it.atZone(timeZone.zoneId) }
153→                is DateTimeUnit.DayBased ->
154→                    thisZdt.plusDays(safeMultiply(value, unit.days.toLong())).toInstant()
155→                is DateTimeUnit.MonthBased ->
156→                    thisZdt.plusMonths(safeMultiply(value, unit.months.toLong())).toInstant()
157→            }.let(::Instant)
158→        } catch (e: Exception) {
159→            if (e !is DateTimeException && e !is ArithmeticException) throw e
160→            throw DateTimeArithmeticException("Instant $this cannot be represented as local date when adding $value $unit to it", e)
161→        }
162→
163→public actual fun Instant.plus(value: Long, unit: DateTimeUnit.TimeBased): Instant =
164→    try {
165→        multiplyAndDivide(value, unit.nanoseconds, NANOS_PER_ONE.toLong()).let { (d, r) ->
166→            Instant(this.value.plusSeconds(d).plusNanos(r))
167→        }
168→    } catch (e: Exception) {
169→        if (e !is DateTimeException && e !is ArithmeticException) throw e
170→        if (value > 0) Instant.MAX else Instant.MIN
171→    }
172→
173→public actual fun Instant.periodUntil(other: Instant, timeZone: TimeZone): DateTimePeriod {
174→    var thisZdt = this.atZone(timeZone)
175→    val otherZdt = other.atZone(timeZone)
176→
177→    val months = thisZdt.until(otherZdt, ChronoUnit.MONTHS); thisZdt = thisZdt.plusMonths(months)
178→    val days = thisZdt.until(otherZdt, ChronoUnit.DAYS); thisZdt = thisZdt.plusDays(days)
179→    val nanoseconds = thisZdt.until(otherZdt, ChronoUnit.NANOS)
180→
181→    if (months > Int.MAX_VALUE || months < Int.MIN_VALUE) {
182→        throw DateTimeArithmeticException("The number of months between $this and $other does not fit in an Int")
183→    }
184→    return buildDateTimePeriod(months.toInt(), days.toInt(), nanoseconds)
185→}
186→
187→public actual fun Instant.until(other: Instant, unit: DateTimeUnit, timeZone: TimeZone): Long = try {
188→    val thisZdt = this.atZone(timeZone)
189→    val otherZdt = other.atZone(timeZone)
190→    when(unit) {
191→        is DateTimeUnit.TimeBased -> until(other, unit)
192→        is DateTimeUnit.DayBased -> thisZdt.until(otherZdt, ChronoUnit.DAYS) / unit.days
193→        is DateTimeUnit.MonthBased -> thisZdt.until(otherZdt, ChronoUnit.MONTHS) / unit.months
194→    }
195→} catch (e: DateTimeException) {
196→    throw DateTimeArithmeticException(e)
197→} catch (e: ArithmeticException) {
198→    if (this.value < other.value) Long.MAX_VALUE else Long.MIN_VALUE
199→}

  9→package kotlinx.datetime
 10→
 11→import kotlinx.datetime.format.*
 12→import kotlinx.datetime.internal.*
 13→import kotlinx.datetime.serializers.InstantIso8601Serializer
 14→import kotlinx.serialization.Serializable
 15→import kotlin.time.*
 16→import kotlin.time.Duration.Companion.nanoseconds
 17→import kotlin.time.Duration.Companion.seconds
 18→
 19→public actual enum class DayOfWeek {
 20→    MONDAY,
 21→    TUESDAY,
 22→    WEDNESDAY,
 23→    THURSDAY,
 24→    FRIDAY,
 25→    SATURDAY,
 26→    SUNDAY;
 27→}
 28→
 29→/**
 30→ * The minimum supported epoch second.
 31→ */
 32→private const val MIN_SECOND = -31619119219200L // -1000000-01-01T00:00:00Z
 33→
 34→/**
 35→ * The maximum supported epoch second.
 36→ */
 37→private const val MAX_SECOND = 31494816403199L // +1000000-12-31T23:59:59
 38→
 39→private fun isValidInstantSecond(second: Long) = second >= MIN_SECOND && second <= MAX_SECOND
 40→
 41→@Serializable(with = InstantIso8601Serializer::class)
 42→public actual class Instant internal constructor(public actual val epochSeconds: Long, public actual val nanosecondsOfSecond: Int) : Comparable<Instant> {
 43→
 44→    init {
 45→        require(isValidInstantSecond(epochSeconds)) { "Instant exceeds minimum or maximum instant" }
 46→    }
 47→
 48→    // org.threeten.bp.Instant#toEpochMilli
 49→    public actual fun toEpochMilliseconds(): Long =
 50→        epochSeconds * MILLIS_PER_ONE + nanosecondsOfSecond / NANOS_PER_MILLI
 51→
 52→    // org.threeten.bp.Instant#plus(long, long)
 53→    /**
 54→     * @throws ArithmeticException if arithmetic overflow occurs
 55→     * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the boundaries of Instant are overflown
 56→     */
 57→    internal fun plus(secondsToAdd: Long, nanosToAdd: Long): Instant {
 58→        if ((secondsToAdd or nanosToAdd) == 0L) {
 59→            return this
 60→        }
 61→        val newEpochSeconds: Long = safeAdd(safeAdd(epochSeconds, secondsToAdd), (nanosToAdd / NANOS_PER_ONE))
 62→        val newNanosToAdd = nanosToAdd % NANOS_PER_ONE
 63→        val nanoAdjustment = (nanosecondsOfSecond + newNanosToAdd) // safe int+NANOS_PER_ONE
 64→        return fromEpochSecondsThrowing(newEpochSeconds, nanoAdjustment)
 65→    }
 66→
 67→    public actual operator fun plus(duration: Duration): Instant = duration.toComponents { secondsToAdd, nanosecondsToAdd ->
 68→        try {
 69→            plus(secondsToAdd, nanosecondsToAdd.toLong())
 70→        } catch (e: IllegalArgumentException) {
 71→            if (duration.isPositive()) MAX else MIN
 72→        } catch (e: ArithmeticException) {
 73→            if (duration.isPositive()) MAX else MIN
 74→        }
 75→    }
 76→
 77→    public actual operator fun minus(duration: Duration): Instant = plus(-duration)
 78→
 79→    public actual operator fun minus(other: Instant): Duration =
 80→        (this.epochSeconds - other.epochSeconds).seconds + // won't overflow given the instant bounds
 81→        (this.nanosecondsOfSecond - other.nanosecondsOfSecond).nanoseconds
 82→
 83→    actual override fun compareTo(other: Instant): Int {
 84→        val s = epochSeconds.compareTo(other.epochSeconds)
 85→        if (s != 0) {
 86→            return s
 87→        }
 88→        return nanosecondsOfSecond.compareTo(other.nanosecondsOfSecond)
 89→    }
 90→
 91→    override fun equals(other: Any?): Boolean =
 92→        this === other || other is Instant && this.epochSeconds == other.epochSeconds && this.nanosecondsOfSecond == other.nanosecondsOfSecond
 93→
 94→    // org.threeten.bp.Instant#hashCode
 95→    override fun hashCode(): Int =
 96→        (epochSeconds xor (epochSeconds ushr 32)).toInt() + 51 * nanosecondsOfSecond
 97→
 98→    // org.threeten.bp.format.DateTimeFormatterBuilder.InstantPrinterParser#print
 99→    actual override fun toString(): String = format(ISO_DATE_TIME_OFFSET_WITH_TRAILING_ZEROS)
100→
101→    public actual companion object {
102→        internal actual val MIN = Instant(MIN_SECOND, 0)
103→        internal actual val MAX = Instant(MAX_SECOND, 999_999_999)
104→
105→        @Deprecated("Use Clock.System.now() instead", ReplaceWith("Clock.System.now()", "kotlinx.datetime.Clock"), level = DeprecationLevel.ERROR)
106→        public actual fun now(): Instant = currentTime()
107→
108→        // org.threeten.bp.Instant#ofEpochMilli
109→        public actual fun fromEpochMilliseconds(epochMilliseconds: Long): Instant =
110→            if (epochMilliseconds < MIN_SECOND * MILLIS_PER_ONE) MIN
111→            else if (epochMilliseconds > MAX_SECOND * MILLIS_PER_ONE) MAX
112→            else Instant(
113→                epochMilliseconds.floorDiv(MILLIS_PER_ONE.toLong()),
114→                (epochMilliseconds.mod(MILLIS_PER_ONE.toLong()) * NANOS_PER_MILLI).toInt()
115→            )
116→
117→        /**
118→         * @throws ArithmeticException if arithmetic overflow occurs
119→         * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the boundaries of Instant are overflown
120→         */
121→        private fun fromEpochSecondsThrowing(epochSeconds: Long, nanosecondAdjustment: Long): Instant {
122→            val secs = safeAdd(epochSeconds, nanosecondAdjustment.floorDiv(NANOS_PER_ONE.toLong()))
123→            val nos = nanosecondAdjustment.mod(NANOS_PER_ONE.toLong()).toInt()
124→            return Instant(secs, nos)
125→        }
126→
127→        // org.threeten.bp.Instant#ofEpochSecond(long, long)
128→        public actual fun fromEpochSeconds(epochSeconds: Long, nanosecondAdjustment: Long): Instant =
129→            try {
130→                fromEpochSecondsThrowing(epochSeconds, nanosecondAdjustment)
131→            } catch (e: ArithmeticException) {
132→                if (epochSeconds > 0) MAX else MIN
133→            } catch (e: IllegalArgumentException) {
134→                if (epochSeconds > 0) MAX else MIN
135→            }
136→
137→        public actual fun fromEpochSeconds(epochSeconds: Long, nanosecondAdjustment: Int): Instant =
138→            fromEpochSeconds(epochSeconds, nanosecondAdjustment.toLong())
139→
140→        public actual fun parse(input: CharSequence, format: DateTimeFormat<DateTimeComponents>): Instant = try {
141→            format.parse(input).toInstantUsingOffset()
142→        } catch (e: IllegalArgumentException) {
143→            throw DateTimeFormatException("Failed to parse an instant from '$input'", e)
144→        }
145→
146→        @Deprecated("This overload is only kept for binary compatibility", level = DeprecationLevel.HIDDEN)
147→        public fun parse(isoString: String): Instant = parse(input = isoString)
148→
149→        public actual val DISTANT_PAST: Instant = fromEpochSeconds(DISTANT_PAST_SECONDS, 999_999_999)
150→
151→        public actual val DISTANT_FUTURE: Instant = fromEpochSeconds(DISTANT_FUTURE_SECONDS, 0)
152→    }
153→
154→}
155→
156→private fun Instant.toZonedDateTimeFailing(zone: TimeZone): ZonedDateTime = try {
157→    toZonedDateTime(zone)
158→} catch (e: IllegalArgumentException) {
159→    throw DateTimeArithmeticException("Can not convert instant $this to LocalDateTime to perform computations", e)
160→}
161→
162→/**
163→ * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the [Instant] exceeds the boundaries of [LocalDateTime]
164→ */
165→private fun Instant.toZonedDateTime(zone: TimeZone): ZonedDateTime {
166→    val currentOffset = zone.offsetAt(this)
167→    return ZonedDateTime(toLocalDateTimeImpl(currentOffset), zone, currentOffset)
168→}
169→
170→/** Check that [Instant] fits in [ZonedDateTime].
171→ * This is done on the results of computations for consistency with other platforms.
172→ */
173→private fun Instant.check(zone: TimeZone): Instant = this@check.also {
174→    toZonedDateTimeFailing(zone)
175→}
176→
177→public actual fun Instant.plus(period: DateTimePeriod, timeZone: TimeZone): Instant = try {
178→    with(period) {
179→        val withDate = toZonedDateTimeFailing(timeZone)
180→            .run { if (totalMonths != 0) plus(totalMonths, DateTimeUnit.MONTH) else this }
181→            .run { if (days != 0) plus(days, DateTimeUnit.DAY) else this }
182→        withDate.toInstant()
183→            .run { if (totalNanoseconds != 0L) plus(0, totalNanoseconds).check(timeZone) else this }
184→    }.check(timeZone)
185→} catch (e: ArithmeticException) {
186→    throw DateTimeArithmeticException("Arithmetic overflow when adding CalendarPeriod to an Instant", e)
187→} catch (e: IllegalArgumentException) {
188→    throw DateTimeArithmeticException("Boundaries of Instant exceeded when adding CalendarPeriod", e)
189→}
190→
191→@Deprecated("Use the plus overload with an explicit number of units", ReplaceWith("this.plus(1, unit, timeZone)"))
192→public actual fun Instant.plus(unit: DateTimeUnit, timeZone: TimeZone): Instant =
193→    plus(1L, unit, timeZone)
194→public actual fun Instant.plus(value: Int, unit: DateTimeUnit, timeZone: TimeZone): Instant =
195→    plus(value.toLong(), unit, timeZone)
196→public actual fun Instant.minus(value: Int, unit: DateTimeUnit, timeZone: TimeZone): Instant =
197→    plus(-value.toLong(), unit, timeZone)
198→public actual fun Instant.plus(value: Long, unit: DateTimeUnit, timeZone: TimeZone): Instant = try {
199→    when (unit) {
200→        is DateTimeUnit.DateBased -> {
201→            if (value < Int.MIN_VALUE || value > Int.MAX_VALUE)
202→                throw ArithmeticException("Can't add a Long date-based value, as it would cause an overflow")
203→            toZonedDateTimeFailing(timeZone).plus(value.toInt(), unit).toInstant()
204→        }
205→        is DateTimeUnit.TimeBased ->
206→            check(timeZone).plus(value, unit).check(timeZone)
207→    }
208→} catch (e: ArithmeticException) {
209→    throw DateTimeArithmeticException("Arithmetic overflow when adding to an Instant", e)
210→} catch (e: IllegalArgumentException) {
211→    throw DateTimeArithmeticException("Boundaries of Instant exceeded when adding a value", e)
212→}
213→
214→public actual fun Instant.plus(value: Long, unit: DateTimeUnit.TimeBased): Instant =
215→    try {
216→        multiplyAndDivide(value, unit.nanoseconds, NANOS_PER_ONE.toLong()).let { (seconds, nanoseconds) ->
217→            plus(seconds, nanoseconds)
218→        }
219→    } catch (e: ArithmeticException) {
220→        if (value > 0) Instant.MAX else Instant.MIN
221→    } catch (e: IllegalArgumentException) {
222→        if (value > 0) Instant.MAX else Instant.MIN
223→    }
224→
225→public actual fun Instant.periodUntil(other: Instant, timeZone: TimeZone): DateTimePeriod {
226→    var thisLdt = toZonedDateTimeFailing(timeZone)
227→    val otherLdt = other.toZonedDateTimeFailing(timeZone)
228→
229→    val months = thisLdt.until(otherLdt, DateTimeUnit.MONTH).toInt() // `until` on dates never fails
230→    thisLdt = thisLdt.plus(months, DateTimeUnit.MONTH) // won't throw: thisLdt + months <= otherLdt, which is known to be valid
231→    val days = thisLdt.until(otherLdt, DateTimeUnit.DAY).toInt() // `until` on dates never fails
232→    thisLdt = thisLdt.plus(days, DateTimeUnit.DAY) // won't throw: thisLdt + days <= otherLdt
233→    val nanoseconds = thisLdt.until(otherLdt, DateTimeUnit.NANOSECOND) // |otherLdt - thisLdt| < 24h
234→
235→    return buildDateTimePeriod(months, days, nanoseconds)
236→}
237→
238→public actual fun Instant.until(other: Instant, unit: DateTimeUnit, timeZone: TimeZone): Long =
239→    when (unit) {
240→        is DateTimeUnit.DateBased ->
241→            toZonedDateTimeFailing(timeZone).dateTime.until(other.toZonedDateTimeFailing(timeZone).dateTime, unit)
242→                .toLong()
243→        is DateTimeUnit.TimeBased -> {
244→            check(timeZone); other.check(timeZone)
245→            until(other, unit)
246→        }
247→    }
248→
249→private val ISO_DATE_TIME_OFFSET_WITH_TRAILING_ZEROS = DateTimeComponents.Format {
250→    date(ISO_DATE)
251→    alternativeParsing({
252→        char('t')
253→    }) {
254→        char('T')
255→    }
256→    hour()
257→    char(':')
258→    minute()
259→    char(':')
260→    second()
261→    optional {
262→        char('.')
263→        secondFractionInternal(1, 9, FractionalSecondDirective.GROUP_BY_THREE)
264→    }
265→    isoOffset(
266→        zOnZero = true,
267→        useSeparator = true,
268→        outputMinute = WhenToOutput.IF_NONZERO,
269→        outputSecond = WhenToOutput.IF_NONZERO
270→    )
271→}
272→
